parentQuestion: A patient cannot dorsiflex their foot after a fibular neck fracture. The foot slaps the ground during walking (steppage gait). Which nerve runs around the fibular neck and is most likely injured?

scaffolds.q: Dorsiflexion means lifting your foot and toes upward (toward your shin).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Dorsiflexion points your toes up. You need dorsiflexion to clear your foot off the ground when walking. Without it, your foot drags.

scaffolds.q: If you can't dorsiflex your foot, it hangs down (foot drop).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Without dorsiflexor muscles working, the foot drops into plantarflexion (pointing down). This makes walking very difficult.

scaffolds.q: The fibula is the thin bone on the outside of your lower leg.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The fibula is the smaller bone, lateral to the tibia. Near the knee, its top portion (fibular neck) is an important landmark where a nerve wraps around.

scaffolds.q: The common fibular nerve wraps around the fibular neck just below the knee.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The common fibular (peroneal) nerve loops around the fibular neck superficially. This exposed location makes it very vulnerable to injury.

scaffolds.q: The common fibular nerve controls the muscles that lift the foot.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The common fibular nerve gives rise to nerves that control foot dorsiflexion (lifting foot up) and eversion (turning sole outward).

scaffolds.q: 'Foot drop' is when the foot hangs down because the dorsiflexors are paralyzed.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Without dorsiflexor function, gravity pulls the foot down. The patient must lift the knee high to clear the dropped foot - this is called 'steppage gait.'

scaffolds.q: Steppage gait means lifting the knee high to clear a dropped foot when walking.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! When you can't lift your foot, you must lift your whole leg higher than normal. The foot then slaps the ground because you can't control the landing.

scaffolds.q: The common fibular nerve is the most commonly injured nerve in the lower limb.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Because it wraps superficially around the bony fibular neck, the common fibular nerve is easily damaged by fractures, casts, leg crossing, or direct trauma.

scaffolds.q: Foot drop after fibular neck fracture indicates common fibular nerve injury.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The combination of fibular neck injury + foot drop is classic for common fibular nerve damage. The nerve wraps right around this bone - fracture it, and the nerve gets crushed.
